danger_signals.visual_form: Unnamed units with no national affiliation on map
danger_signals.meaning: Uncontrolled threat emanating from unclaimed huts, unexplored land, or sea; decreases as exploration progresses

danger_signals.visual_form: Computer-controlled civilization units massing at borders
danger_signals.meaning: Potential warfare threat; indicates aggressive diplomatic posture

danger_signals.visual_form: Opponent civilizations with advanced units unavailable to player
danger_signals.meaning: Strategic disadvantage; indicates need for research acceleration or military caution

safety_signals.visual_form: Fog of war lifted from map sections
safety_signals.meaning: Known land with reduced barbarian spawn likelihood; territorial security increases with exploration

safety_signals.visual_form: Placed city markers with improvements constructed
safety_signals.meaning: Permanent territorial control and resource generation; infrastructure stability

safety_signals.visual_form: Treaty agreements with named civilization leaders
safety_signals.meaning: Formal non-aggression pact; reduces immediate conflict risk

Display fiercely expansionist traits; generally extremely wealthy; distinct AI behavior patterns
Named leader and city names distinguish faction

Display distinct historical traits; named leader and city names distinguish faction

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Empty world map with limited unit options (settler units only); basic technology tree (pottery, wheel, alphabet)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: High barbarian spawn rate from unclaimed territory; limited trade and diplomatic options

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Populated map with multiple civilizations, established cities, road networks connecting settlements
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Barbarian threats decrease; diplomatic relationships form; military units become diverse; technological race evident

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Late game (Industrial Age through Space Age)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dense urbanization, railroad networks, advanced military units on map; visual representation of space-age technologies
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Barbarian threats nearly eliminated; diplomatic alliances or wars entrenched; victory conditions become apparent (space race, military dominance, cultural/scientific superiority)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Fog of war covers unexplored map sections
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Barbarians can spawn from unseen huts and unclaimed land; strategic uncertainty drives military/defensive posture

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: City placement marks claim; road/railroad networks establish control; visual borders implied by continuous city presence
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Barbarian spawning prevented; resource extraction begins; other civilizations recognize territorial boundaries

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Access to advanced unit types unavailable to opponents; upgraded city improvements visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Military superiority enables aggressive expansion; diplomatic leverage increases; trade agreements favor tech-leading civ

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Government type affects city visual presentation (taxation display, workforce allocation UI)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Tax rate adjustments influence citizen happiness; research priority changes affect unit/improvement production

environmental_storytelling.example: Indian cities have Indian names; Egyptian cities have Egyptian names; reinforces historical identity and faction ownership
Each civilization has unique musical theme associated with its leader; audio cue identifies diplomatic contact or unit activity
Mines, irrigation, and roads physically alter landscape appearance; shows player effort and terraforming investment over time
Early units are basic (settlers, warriors); advancement unlocks mounted knights, cannons, tanks; visual unit evolution mirrors tech tree
Earth map mode starts civilizations at historical locations; Americans, Egyptians, etc. spawn near their actual historical homelands
Unnamed, unaffiliated units with no leader; visual absence of faction identity marks them as chaos/instability rather than organized threat

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Units remain within borders; diplomatic overtures accepted; trade routes established; no unit massing at shared borders
Units concentrate at player civilization borders; repeated diplomatic demands; military unit construction accelerates visibly
Units actively attack player settlements and armies; siege behavior around cities; raiding parties target resource nodes
Stationary within or near existing city; awaits orders for new city foundation or terrain improvement
Movement toward unclaimed territory; fog of war lifts in path of unit; retreats if barbarians encountered
Positioned near player cities; remains in territory; engages only if threatened; garrison-like positioning
Moves toward enemy territory; ignores hazards; concentrates with other units for siege operations
Spawns from huts in unexplored territory; attacks nearby units and cities indiscriminately; no allegiance signal; declines as exploration progresses

notes: Civilization SNES uses primarily cosmetic faction differentiation through civilization selection. Color assignment, leader naming, city names, and musical heralds provide faction identity rather than distinct visual unit designs. The core manifestation system relies on map state (explored/unexplored), unit type progression tied to technology, and territorial control visualization. World state is communicated primarily through technology availability and unit access rather than dramatic environmental shifts. Danger and safety are signaled through fog of war coverage, barbarian presence/absence, and unit concentration patterns rather than explicit visual warning systems. Political situation is inferred through available diplomatic options and leader behavior rather than visual representations of alliances or enmity.
